title: Listening - Part 4: Downsizing Your Home
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
topic: five people discussing their decisions to move from a large family home to a much smaller property

content.audio_script: Speaker 1: I’d lived in my four-bedroom house for thirty years, but [21]once my children had all moved out, I realized I was spending all my time and money just cleaning and heating empty rooms[/21]. It felt like a burden. I moved to a small city apartment. [26]The most rewarding aspect has been the incredible sense of freedom that comes with having fewer possessions[/26]. I had to declutter everything, and it was a revelation. I feel much lighter and more spontaneous now, as I’m no longer tied down by 'stuff'. It’s given me a whole new lease on life. || Speaker 2: We moved because [22]we wanted to free up some capital to help our daughter with a deposit for her own first home[/22]. It was a purely practical, financial decision. We bought a small cottage in a nearby village. [27]What I found most difficult was the emotional toll of leaving the house where we’d raised our family[/27]. Every corner was full of memories. It took a long time for the new place to feel like 'home', and I still find myself accidentally driving toward the old house after work sometimes. It’s been a hard lesson in the difference between a building and a home. || Speaker 3: I’m a writer, and [23]I chose to move to a tiny studio apartment because I wanted to simplify my life and eliminate any distractions from my work[/23]. I wanted to be more focused. [28]The most unexpected benefit has been the dramatic improvement in my social life[/28]. Because my place is so small, I’m forced to go out to cafes and libraries to work and meet people. I’ve become a regular at my local coffee shop and have made so many new friends. I used to be quite isolated in my big house, but now I feel like I’m part of the community. || Speaker 4: I’d recently retired and [24]I moved to a smaller, modern apartment primarily because I wanted to be closer to the city's cultural amenities like theaters and galleries[/24]. I was tired of being in the suburbs. [29]The biggest hurdle has been the lack of outdoor space and the loss of my garden[/29], which I’d spent years cultivating. I’ve had to learn to enjoy balcony gardening, which is a bit of a struggle, but the trade-off for being able to walk to a concert or an exhibition in ten minutes is definitely worth it. || Speaker 5: I’d been feeling increasingly overwhelmed by the maintenance of my old property, so [25]I moved to a smaller place because I wanted to reduce my daily chores and have more time for my own hobbies[/25]. I wanted a lazy retirement! [30]What I value most now is the complete lack of financial stress regarding my monthly bills[/30]. My heating and tax costs have halved, which means I can actually afford to travel and take the classes I’ve always wanted to. It’s given me a sense of security and abundance that I haven't felt for years, and I only wish I’d done it sooner.

task1.title: Task One
task1.instruction: For questions 21–25, choose from the list (A–H) each speaker's main reason for downsizing.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 realized she was spending too much time and money on empty rooms.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 wanted to free up capital to help his daughter buy a home.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 wanted to simplify her life and eliminate distractions from her writing.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 wanted to be closer to theaters and galleries in the city.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 wanted to reduce chores and have more time for hobbies.

options.A: to assist a family member financially
options.B: to live closer to their place of work
options.C: to have better access to local culture
options.D: to move to a more prestigious neighborhood
options.E: to lower their daily domestic workload
options.F: to address the inefficiency of their current home
options.H: to minimize the interruptions to their career

task2.title: Task Two
task2.instruction: For questions 26–30, choose from the list (A–H) what each speaker mentions as an outcome of the move.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: Speaker 1 mentions the incredible sense of freedom from having fewer possessions.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: Speaker 2 highlights the emotional toll of leaving the family home and its memories.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: Speaker 3 mentions a dramatic improvement in her social life as she's forced to go out more.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: Speaker 4 mentions the loss of her garden as the biggest hurdle.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: Speaker 5 values the complete lack of financial stress regarding monthly bills.

options.A: the difficulty of living without a private garden
options.B: a sense of regret about the location chosen
options.C: the discovery of a new local interest
options.D: the psychological benefit of owning less
options.E: an improvement in their physical health
options.F: a significant reduction in their outgoings
options.G: struggling with the sentimental loss of the past
options.H: the development of more social connections
